characters.name: Unnamed Narrator
characters.role: A lawyer and former Master in Chancery who employs Bartleby.

characters.name: Bartleby
characters.role: A pale, sedentary scrivener who eventually refuses to perform any work.

characters.name: John Jacob Astor
characters.role: A wealthy man and former client of the narrator.

characters.name: Turkey
characters.role: An elderly English copyist who becomes rash and erratic in the afternoons.

characters.name: Nippers
characters.role: A young, ambitious copyist suffering from indigestion and irritability.

characters.name: Ginger Nut
characters.role: A twelve-year-old office boy and errand runner.

characters.name: Ginger Nut's Father
characters.role: A carman who sent his son to the office as a student at law.

characters.name: The Dun
characters.role: A man posing as Nippers' client who is actually a debt collector.

characters.name: The Cleaning Woman
characters.role: A woman residing in the attic who cleans the narrator's chambers.

characters.name: The Lawyer Tenant
characters.role: A tenant at No.—Wall-street who is distressed by Bartleby's presence.

characters.name: The Landlord
characters.role: The portly owner of the building at No.—Wall-street.

characters.name: Mr. Cutlets
characters.role: A prison food provider at the Tombs.

characters.name: Monroe Edwards
characters.role: A known forger mentioned by the grub-man.

relationships.evidence: Ginger Nut, the third on my list, was a lad some twelve years old. His father was a carman, ambitious of seeing his son on the bench instead of a cart, before he died. So he sent him to my office as student at law

evidence_spans.text_snippet: Ginger Nut, the third on my list, was a lad some twelve years old. His father was a carman, ambitious of seeing his son on the bench instead of a cart, before he died. So he sent him to my office as student at law
evidence_spans.reason: Explicitly identifies the character as Ginger Nut's father and refers to Ginger Nut as his son.

relationships.evidence: May Mrs. Cutlets and I have the pleasure of your company to dinner, sir, in Mrs. Cutlets’ private room?

evidence_spans.text_snippet: May Mrs. Cutlets and I have the pleasure of your company to dinner, sir, in Mrs. Cutlets’ private room?
evidence_spans.reason: The grub-man invites the narrator to dinner with Mrs. Cutlets, and the provided character list explicitly defines her as the wife of the grub-man.

relationships.evidence: In Chunk 22, when the grub-man asks 'Is that your friend?', the narrator responds 'Yes.' Additionally, in Chunk 7, the narrator reflects on his desire 'To befriend Bartleby; to humor him in his strange willfulness'.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: To befriend Bartleby; to humor him in his strange willfulness, will cost me little or nothing
evidence_spans.reason: Narrator expresses an explicit intention to establish a friendly bond.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: "Is that your friend?" "Yes."
evidence_spans.reason: Narrator explicitly identifies Bartleby as his friend to a third party.

relationships.temporal_note: The narrator's feeling of friendship is primarily driven by pity and a desire for self-approval, and is not reciprocated by Bartleby.

relationships.evidence: Nipper and I dined together to-day.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: Nipper and I dined together to-day.
evidence_spans.reason: Explicit mention of sharing a meal outside of work duties indicates a social relationship beyond professional requirements.

relationships.evidence: I was not unemployed in my profession by the late John Jacob Astor

evidence_spans.text_snippet: I was not unemployed in my profession by the late John Jacob Astor
evidence_spans.reason: Direct statement of professional employment/client relationship.

relationships.evidence: The narrator employs Turkey as a copyist in his chambers, valuing his morning services and referring to him as his 'right-hand man'.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: valuing his morning services as I did... he need not come to my chambers after twelve o’clock
evidence_spans.reason: Establishes employment in the narrator's chambers.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: “With submission, sir,” said Turkey on this occasion, “I consider myself your right-hand man.”
evidence_spans.reason: Turkey acknowledges his professional role relative to the narrator.

relationships.evidence: At the period just preceding the advent of Bartleby, I had two persons as copyists in my employment... second, Nippers

evidence_spans.text_snippet: I had two persons as copyists in my employment... second, Nippers
evidence_spans.reason: Explicit statement of employment.

relationships.evidence: I had... a promising lad as an office-boy... Ginger Nut... sent him to my office as student at law, errand boy, and cleaner and sweeper

evidence_spans.text_snippet: a promising lad as an office-boy... Ginger Nut
evidence_spans.reason: Identifies Ginger Nut as the office boy.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: sent him to my office as student at law, errand boy, and cleaner and sweeper
evidence_spans.reason: Explicit description of his professional roles.

relationships.evidence: Was there any other thing in which I could procure myself to be ignominiously repulsed by this lean, penniless wight?—my hired clerk? ... he copied for me at the usual rate of four cents a folio

evidence_spans.text_snippet: my hired clerk
evidence_spans.reason: Explicitly identifies Bartleby as the narrator's employee.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: he copied for me at the usual rate of four cents a folio
evidence_spans.reason: Establishes the contractual payment arrangement for work.

relationships.evidence: The narrator turns to both 'Turkey and Nippers' in his chambers to discuss Bartleby's performance.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: turning upon Turkey and Nippers, exclaimed in an excited manner
evidence_spans.reason: They are grouped together as the staff of the office.

relationships.evidence: Not the least among the employments of Ginger Nut... was his duty as cake and apple purveyor for Turkey and Nippers

evidence_spans.text_snippet: his duty as cake and apple purveyor for Turkey and Nippers
evidence_spans.reason: Ginger Nut performs professional duties for Turkey as part of their shared workplace.

relationships.evidence: Not the least among the employments of Ginger Nut... was his duty as cake and apple purveyor for Turkey and Nippers

evidence_spans.text_snippet: his duty as cake and apple purveyor for Turkey and Nippers
evidence_spans.reason: Ginger Nut performs professional duties for Nippers as part of their shared workplace.

relationships.evidence: glad to have among my corps of copyists a man of so singularly sedaate an aspect, which I thought might operate beneficially upon the flighty temper of Turkey

evidence_spans.text_snippet: glad to have among my corps of copyists... operate beneficially upon the flighty temper of Turkey
evidence_spans.reason: Bartleby is hired into the same 'corps of copyists' as Turkey.

relationships.evidence: glad to have among my corps of copyists a man of so singularly sedaate an aspect, which I thought might operate beneficially upon... the fiery one of Nippers

evidence_spans.text_snippet: glad to have among my corps of copyists... operate beneficially upon... the fiery one of Nippers
evidence_spans.reason: Bartleby is hired into the same 'corps of copyists' as Nippers.

relationships.evidence: engaged him, glad to have among my corps of copyists

evidence_spans.text_snippet: I engaged him, glad to have among my corps of copyists a man of so singularly sedaate an aspect
evidence_spans.reason: Both are employees of the narrator.

relationships.evidence: one individual who called upon him at my chambers, and who, with a grand air, he insisted was his client, was no other than a dun

evidence_spans.text_snippet: one individual who called upon him at my chambers, and who, with a grand air, he insisted was his client, was no other than a dun
evidence_spans.reason: Nippers frames the relationship as client-service, regardless of the Narrator's suspicions of fraud.

relationships.evidence: Nippers insists that the man (who is actually a debt collector) is his client.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: one individual who called upon him at my chambers, and who, with a grand air, he insisted was his client, was no other than a dun
evidence_spans.reason: Establishes a perceived client-service relationship.

relationships.evidence: One was kept by a woman residing in the attic, which person weekly scrubbed and daily swept and dusted my apartments

evidence_spans.text_snippet: One was kept by a woman residing in the attic, which person weekly scrubbed and daily swept and dusted my apartments.
evidence_spans.reason: The woman provides cleaning services for the narrator's office.

relationships.evidence: whom I knew to be the landlord of No.—Wall-street

evidence_spans.text_snippet: whom I knew to be the landlord of No.—Wall-street
evidence_spans.reason: Establishes a tenant-landlord relationship as the narrator's office was located in the building.

relationships.evidence: I want you to give particular attention to my friend there; let him have the best dinner you can get. And you must be as polite to him as possible. ... slipping some silver into the grub-man’s hands

evidence_spans.text_snippet: slipping some silver into the grub-man’s hands (for so they called him). “I want you to give particular attention to my friend there; let him have the best dinner you can get.
evidence_spans.reason: The narrator hires the grub-man to provide food for Bartlebby.

relationships.temporal_note: Temporary interaction during narrator's visit to the Tombs.

relationships.evidence: Such gentlemen as have friends here, hire me to provide them with something good to eat

evidence_spans.text_snippet: Such gentlemen as have friends here, hire me to provide them with something good to eat
evidence_spans.reason: The grub-man is hired to provide food to BartlebY.

relationships.evidence: These gentlemen, my tenants

evidence_spans.text_snippet: These gentlemen, my tenants, cannot stand it any longer; Mr. B— pointing to the lawyer
evidence_spans.reason: Both are tenants of the same landlord in the same professional building.

relationships.evidence: His father was a carman... So he sent him to my office as student at law

evidence_spans.text_snippet: His father was a carman, ambitious of seeing his son on the bench instead of a cart, before he died. So he sent him to my office as student at law, errand boy, and cleaner and sweeper, at the rate of one dollar a week.
evidence_spans.reason: The father entered into a professional arrangement with the narrator to train/employ his son.

relationships.evidence: The narrator describes Bartleby as an 'intolerable incubus' and a 'millstone' and feels 'nervous resentment' due to Bartleby's passive resistance and refusal to work or leave the office.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: Nothing so aggravates an earnest person as a passive resistance.
evidence_spans.reason: Establishes the adversarial nature of Bartleby's behavior towards the narrator.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: In plain fact, he had now become a millstone to me, not only useless as a necklace, but afflictive to bear.
evidence_spans.reason: Describes the narrator's perception of Bartleby as a burden/adversary.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: I resolved to gather all my faculties together, and for ever rid me of this intolerable incubus.
evidence_spans.reason: Shows the narrator's active goal to remove Bartleby.

relationships.temporal_note: The relationship evolves from curiosity and pity to irritation and a desire for removal.

relationships.evidence: Nippers expresses a desire to 'kick him out of the office' and directs 'hissing maledictions' toward him.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: "I think I should kick him out of the office."
evidence_spans.reason: Direct statement of hostility.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: Nippers, twitching in his chair with a dyspeptic nervousness, ground out between his set teeth occasional hissing maledictions against the stubborn oaf behind the screen.
evidence_spans.reason: Describes active verbal hostility.

relationships.evidence: Turkey threatens physical violence, stating he wants to 'black his eyes for him.'

evidence_spans.text_snippet: "Think of it?" roared Turkey; "I think I’ll just step behind his screen, and black his eyes for him!"
evidence_spans.reason: Direct threat of physical harm.

relationships.evidence: The landlord views Bartleby as a nuisance that drives away clients and eventually has him arrested and removed to the Tombs as a vagrant.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: "You must take him away, sir, at once," cried a portly person among them... the landlord of No.—Wall-street.
evidence_spans.reason: Demand for removal.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: It informed me that the writer had sent to the police, and had Bartleby removed to the Tombs as a vagrant.
evidence_spans.reason: The landlord takes legal action to forcibly remove the character.

relationships.evidence: the writer had sent to the police, and had Bartleby removed to the Tombs as a vagrant.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: It informed me that the writer had sent to the police, and had Bartleby removed to the Tombs as a vagrant.
evidence_spans.reason: The landlord uses legal authority to have Bartleby forcibly removed and imprisoned.

relationships.evidence: The lawyer tenant (Mr. B—) is distressed by Bartleby's presence and eventually turns him out of his room.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: "Mr. B—" pointing to the lawyer, "has turned him out of his room"
evidence_spans.reason: Action of eviction.

relationships.evidence: Mr. B—” pointing to the lawyer, “has turned him out of his room

evidence_spans.text_snippet: Mr. B—” pointing to the lawyer, “has turned him out of his room
evidence_spans.reason: The lawyer tenant explicitly evicted Bartleby.

relationships.evidence: The Dun is a debt collector who poses as a client to target Nippers.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: I have good reason to believe, however, that one individual who called upon him... was no other than a dun
evidence_spans.reason: The identity of the character as a debt collector indicates a structurally antagonistic relationship.

relationships.evidence: Bartleby, this is Mr. Cutlets; you will find him very useful to you. ... Such gentlemen as have friends here, hire me to provide them with something good to eat.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: “I am the grub-man. Such gentlemen as have friends here, hire me to provide them with something good to eat.”
evidence_spans.reason: The grub-man's role is to provide food services to prisoners who have external funding.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: “Bartleby, this is Mr. Cutlets; you will find him very useful to you.”
evidence_spans.reason: The grub-man is introduced as a service provider for Bartlebby.

relationships.temporal_note: Temporary professional relationship established by the narrator.

relationships.evidence: Ginger Nut's father arranged for his son to be sent to the narrator's office as a student at law and employee.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: His father was a carman... So he sent him to my office as student at law, errand boy, and cleaner and sweeper, at the rate of one dollar a week.
evidence_spans.reason: Indicates a contractual/institutional arrangement between the father and the employer.

relationships.evidence: These gentlemen, my tenants, cannot stand it any longer; Mr. B— pointing to the lawyer

evidence_spans.text_snippet: These gentlemen, my tenants, cannot stand it any longer; Mr. B— pointing to the lawyer
evidence_spans.reason: Explicitly identifies the lawyer tenant as one of the landlord's tenants.

relationships.evidence: the landlord... advancing upon me... 'something you must do, and that without delay.' ... So fearful was I of being again hunted out by the incensed landlord

evidence_spans.text_snippet: cried a portly person among them, advancing upon me, and whom I knew to be the landlord of No.—Wall-street. “These gentlemen, my tenants, cannot stand it any longer... something you must do, and that without delay.”
evidence_spans.reason: The landlord aggressively confronts the narrator, demanding he resolve the Bartleby situation.

evidence_spans.text_snippet: So fearful was I of being again hunted out by the incensed landlord and his exasperated tenants
evidence_spans.reason: The narrator describes the landlord as 'incensed' and describes himself as being 'hunted out'.

relationships.evidence: “Bartleby!” I know you,” he said... “and I want nothing to say to you.”

evidence_spans.text_snippet: “Bartleby!” I know you,” he said, without looking round,—“and I want nothing to say to you.”
evidence_spans.reason: Bartleby explicitly rejects the narrator and expresses a desire for no communication, framing the narrator as unwelcome.

chunks.preview: Title: Bartleby, the Scrivener: A Story of Wall-Street Author: Herman Melville I am a rather elderly man. The nature of my avocations ...
looked upon the white wall of the interior of a spacious sky-light shaft, penetrating the building from top to bottom. This view ...
because, though the civilest, nay, the blandest and most reverential of men in the morning, yet in the afternoon he was disposed, ...
a scrivener’s table altogether. Among the manifestations of his diseased ambition was a fondness he had for receiving visits from certain ambiguous-looking ...
on about twelve o’clock, I never had to do with their eccentricities at one time. Their fits relieved each other like guards. ...
no view at all, though it gave some light. Within three feet of the panes was a wall, and the light came ...
should have violently dismissed him from the premises. But as it was, I should have as soon thought of turning my pale ...
think that you are.” “Nippers,” said I, “what do _you_ think of it?” “I think I should kick him out of the ...
driven forth miserably to starve. Yes. Here I can cheaply purchase a delicious self-approval. To befriend Bartleby; to humor him in his ...
some terrible retribution very close at hand. At the moment I half intended something of the kind. But upon the whole, as ...
saying quietly that he was sorry, but he was deeply engaged just then, and—preferred not admitting me at present. In a brief ...
Adam. I remembered the bright silks and sparkling faces I had seen that day, in gala trim, swan-like sailing down the Mississippi ...
it is perceived that such pity cannot lead to effectual succor, common sense bids the soul rid of it. What I saw ...
to be a little reasonable,” was his mildly cadaverous reply. Just then the folding-doors opened, and Nippers approached. He seemed suffering from ...
I thought that, having nothing else earthly to do, Bartleby would surely be less inflexible than usual, and carry these letters to ...
